title: Position Is machine learning good or bad for the natural sciences
abstract: Machine learning (ML) methods are having a huge impact across all of the sciences. However, ML has a strong ontology-in which only the data exist-and a strong epistemology-in which a model is considered good if it performs well on held-out training data. These philosophies are in strong conflict with both standard practices and key philosophies in the natural sciences. Here we identify some locations for ML in the natural sciences at which the ontology and epistemology are valuable. For example, when an expressive machine learning model is used in a causal inference to represent the effects of confounders, such as foregrounds, backgrounds, or instrument calibration parameters, the model capacity and loose philosophy of ML can make the results more trustworthy. We also show that there are contexts in which the introduction of ML introduces strong, unwanted statistical biases. For one, when ML models are used to emulate physical (or first-principles) simulations, they amplify confirmation biases. For another, when expressive regressions are used to label datasets, those labels cannot be used in downstream joint or ensemble analyses without taking on uncontrolled biases. The question in the title is being asked of all of the natural sciences; that is, we are calling on the scientific communities to take a step back and consider the role and value of ML in their fields; the (partial) answers we give here come from the particular perspective of physics.
impact: This Position Paper is directly aimed at producing certain kinds of broader impacts, in the execution of natural-science investigations and projects. ML has many roles in the natural sciences. This work is intended to start a discussion which, in time, will support the implementation of ML in good roles in scientific projects, and criticize the implementation of ML in less good roles in scientific projects. It is also intended to help practitioners understand and respond to biases introduced by the use of ML in those less-good roles. The goal of this work is to improve the accuracy and success of the natural sciences. However, if the recommendations in this Position Paper are adopted by scientific communities, the costs of some scientific projects may increase.

query_input.scenario: Training expressive models on heterogeneous scientific datasets
query_input.issue: SGD optimization can transiently favor majority subpopulations, skewing model insights.
hazard: Expressive ML models for scientific inference amplify confirmation bias and statistical biases through unvalidated flexibility and selective held-out evaluation.
exposure: Researchers analyzing model behavior across subpopulations.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Researchers' reliance on held-out aggregate performance metrics obscures subpopulation-specific biases, leaving them unable to detect skewed model behavior.
hipporag_result.impact: Scientific knowledge becomes distorted, with minority subpopulations misrepresented and downstream analyses contaminated by uncontrolled biases.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Expressive ML models amplifying biases through unvalidated flexibility and selective held-out evaluation, when researchers analyze subpopulation behavior, trigger reliance on aggregate metrics that obscures subpopulation biases, distorting scientific knowledge and misrepresenting minority subpopulations.

dose_response: Expressive ML models' amplification of statistical biases enables SGD to favor majority patterns, skewing model insights and leading to erroneous subgroup predictions and health disparities.
vulnerability: Standard SGD training procedures can amplify majority subpopulation patterns, overshadowing minority subgroups' signals.
impact: Erroneous subgroup predictions result in inequitable resource allocation and exacerbate health disparities.
